title: Reflection Log
text: Week 2 - Scripts, Functions & Refactoring

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 3 - Debugging

Did you use an LLM to help debug? What did you share with it, and what approach did it take? Was the answer correct? How did you verify it?

I had to fix an error and try the debugging methods. The error was shown on RStudio but I couldn’t see what was wrong. I pasted the code and error message into the LLM and asked it to explain the message and find which line is faulty. After I could fix the error myself.
For testing the debug method I also had the LLM explain the methods first, since I those were new to me. I tried to use the methods myself but couldn’t really figure it out. I asked the LLM on how to actually use them.

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 4 - Version Control & Collaborative

Describe a confusing or frustrating moment with git this week. How did you resolve it? What do you now understand that you didn’t before?

A frustrating moment was when initialising git. The main branch was called mian which was a setting mispell. Additionally the README section was filled with ‘statprog’ 4 times which wasn’t supposed to be there. I tried fixing it with the help of an LLM but it did more trouble than good. So I decided to delete the original repository and start over. The same mistake of the missspelled main branch happened again. I decided to look into the settings and found that the name of the original branch, when creating the repository was set to ‘mian’. I was able to set it to ‘main’ and create the original branch with the right name. I moved on to connecting the repository to my folder. It took me a few tries and hours to fix the mistakes, but I was able to get the repository clean.

How does committing code frequently change how you think about your work? Have you noticed yourself structuring tasks differently because of it?

It makes me think more structured. Completing / Commiting one task first and then moving on to the next one to ensure that the structure doesn’t get lost and all task get done cleanly.

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 5 - Quarto Websites & Collaborative Coding

What is the difference between a Quarto document and a Quarto website project? Describe in your own words what _quarto.yml does and how it changes how you think about organising a multi-file project.

A quarto document is a single page, where as a quarto website is a collection of different quarto documents.
_quarto.yml is the control settings of the whole Quarto website and structures everything. Instead of having to write the settings of the page at the top everytime, it is defined once in the _quarto.yml file. In my opinion, it is much more efficient and gives a structured look since the settings are the same across the website.

Walk through one format: or execute: option you set in _quarto.yml this week. Why did you choose that option, and what effect did it have on the rendered output?

The format is html because I want the output to be a website.
code-fold: true - the code will not be directly shown. The user can click on the expandable to show the code. I chose this because it gives a more clean look instead of having pages of code.
code-summary: “Show code” - This message will appear on the expandable to specify that there is code and an option to show it.
code-line-numbers: true - It shows the line numbers inside of the code chunks. Also for structure.
code-copy: hover - When the user hovers over a code chunk it allows them to copy the code.
theme: - aesthetics of the website

cosmo - theme name
brand - allows custom changes on top of the cosmo theme

css: styles.css - a file that includes specific, custom style changes that are applied to every page
toc: true - Table of contents on the right side

Describe a situation this week where you used a git branch, stash, or pull request. What problem were you solving, and would you approach it the same way again?

I was editing the main proposal file and was working on the main branch. Luckily only I was editing and pushing the changes so there were no conflicts but it could happen that the other teammate works on the branch and then it’ll lead to a merge conflict. To avoid this we decided to use individual branches when working on the project since it will lead to a cleaner history.
++ In week 8 I merged a feature branch into main and deleted the remote branch on GitHub. I continued working on that same branch locally and forgot that the remote tracking was gone. When I tried to push an error message appeared. I was very confused because I didn’t realize the remote branch no longer exists. In my attempt to fix the ‘sync’ issue, I improperly performed a pull that overwrote my local progress. I learned the importance of structured branches and frequent committing and pushing instead of doing the whole project on just one branch and pushing everything at once.
Next time we could resolve that problem like this:

git branch shows I am on main instead of feature branch
git log —oneline --graph --all -n 10 see if commits exist locally
git fetch origin & git log --oneline origin/branchname..branchname check what was pushed –> If list of commits returns it means those commits exist locally and have not been pushed to the remote branch yet
git push -u origin branchname –> push changes from the branch I’m actually on

title: Reflection Log
text:
Week 6 - R Packages

Can you think of packages you have used that are very large or very small? Why do you think they contain many or few functions?
How do the ideas of outside-in and inside-out function design relate to package design?
devtools::load_all() reloads your package without restarting R, whereas source()-ing a file just runs it in the global environment. Did you notice a difference in behaviour when using load_all()? What does it tell you about how packages work differently from plain scripts?
Writing roxygen2 documentation (@param, @returns, @examples) forces you to describe your function from a user’s perspective before you’ve finished writing it. Did documenting a function change how you thought about its interface or arguments?

title: Reflection Log
section: Week 7 - Initial Data Analysis & Data Cleaning
text:
Week 7 - Initial Data Analysis & Data Cleaning

What data format or file type did you encounter that was unfamiliar? How did you read it into R, and what did you learn in the process?

It was unfamiliar that we could just load the data from a different folder inside the project (/data/raw/…). It’s quite logical.

Did you ask an LLM about data licensing, provenance, or quality? What question did you ask, and how did you assess whether the answer was trustworthy?

We didn’t have to ask an LLM since the practical had an example of a licence of a dataset from the same source (Stadt München). We looked into the description and licence section of our Dataset and noticed the two were very similar. So we compared them and it happened to be identical to the licence from the practical. If we do ask LLMs question about our project we make sure to skim the source / information the LLM is refering to. Most of the time we use LLMs to save time so we don’t have to read pages of documentation. The LLM primarely helps find the location of the information.

Describe one visualisation you made during IDA. What did you learn from it that a summary statistic alone wouldn’t have revealed?

We made a visualisation of the unemployment trend over time. It showed us how the rate dropped or increased over the years. While a summary statistic includes the same numbers and one could draw the same conclusions the visualisation shows where the highs and lows were and the overall trend at one glance.

What question did your data raise that you hadn’t anticipated at the start? Do you think it’s worth pursuing, and why?

The data showed an abnormal high rate in the year 2005. The dataset alone couldn’t explain it and so we decided to research. It turned out to be this high because of a reform that counted the unemploymed and people on welfare into one number starting in 2005. After this the new rules were introduced where receiving welfare became stricter and so the numbers decreased again. We deceided to not pursue this question or include it into one of our RQs since it can’t be explained by our data alone and we would have to use sources from outside.

title: Reflection Log
text: Week 9 - Reproducibility, Open Data & renv

title: About
text:
This project analyses unemployment trends across Munich’s 25 city districts from 2000 to 2024, using data from the Munich Open Data Portal. It was completed as the group project for StatProg2 (SoSe 2026) at LMU Munich.
We explore how unemployment percentages have changed over time by city area and if they differ by gender across South Munich’s districts in 2024. Full details on Proposal page.

title: About
text:
This project analyses unemployment trends across Munich’s 25 city districts from 2000 to 2024, using data from the Munich Open Data Portal. It was completed as the group project for StatProg2 (SoSe 2026) at LMU Munich.
We explore how unemployment percentages have changed over time by city area and if they differ by gender across South Munich’s districts in 2024. Full details on Proposal page.

title: About
text:
Group members

Rebecca Patrick - R.Patrick@campus.lmu.de
Anna Chrebtov - Anna.Chrebtov@campus.lmu.de

title: About
text:
Data source and licence
The dataset is published under Datenlizenz Deutschland Namensnennung 2.0 and was downloaded from the Munich Open Data Portal on 2026-05-15.

title: Munich Unemployment Analysis
text:
This project analyses unemployment trends across Munich’s 25 city districts from 2000 to 2024, using open data from the Munich Open Data Portal.
We explore two questions:

How has the unemployment percentage in Munich changed from 2000 to 2024 overall and by city area (North, East, South, West)?
Do unemployment percentages differ in 2024 based on gender across districts in South Munich.

Read the full analysis on the Proposal page or learn more About this project.

title: Proposal
text:
The “Arbeitsmarkt - Arbeitslosen” dataset uses Datenlizenz Deutschland Namensnennung 2.0 (dl-by-de) (Version 2.0). This is a German open government licence: you can copy, use, and redistribute the data for any purpose, including commercially, as long as you attribute the source.



https://www.mstatistik-muenchen.de/indikatorenatlas/export/export.php



Rows: 3250
Columns: 10
Each row represents the yearly percentage of unemployed people per district in Munich, as well as the total yearly percentage across all districts, further divided into total, German, non-German, male, and female categories.
Columns include the id, indicator name, unemployed-person type (total, male, female, …), year, Munich district (including the city total), unemployment percentage, unemployed count, and total resident count (15–64 years old).
Row identification: year (Jahr), district (Raumbezug), and demographic group (Auspragung) together uniquely identify each row.
Key variables we expect to use are the year, district, unemployment percentage, and both base values.



Indikatorwert: the indicator value — not obvious from the name alone that this is the unemployment percentage, or which unit is missing (%).
Basiswert 1 / Basiswert 2: the names give no information about what the values mean.
Auspragung: the column name gives no information about the values it contains — a categorical variable for demographic/subpopulation groups.



The Munich Open Data page gives a formula description but no real variable dictionary. Columns like Auspragung and Basiswert 1 / Basiswert 2 are left unexplained.
A separate metadata PDF from the Statistisches Amt München (linked in the dataset description) reveals information not present in the file itself: Basiswert 2 changes its meaning depending on Auspragung (whether the row concerns gender or nationality), and “Stadt München” rows aggregate geographic data differently than individual district rows.
A user who loads the dataset without tracking down this external PDF could misinterpret the metrics or miscalculate averages by mixing districts and city totals.




How has the unemployment percentage in Munich changed from 2000 to 2024 overall and by city area (North, East, South, West)? → Descriptive
Do unemployment percentages differ in 2024 based on gender across districts in South Munich? → Explanatory

Cut:
We originally considered a third, predictive research question (“Can we build a simple model to predict the unemployment percentage in the next years?”). We cut this to keep scope focused on the two questions above. Also the updated proposal required to lock in at most two focused research questions.



Target audience: Agentur für Arbeit München, Jobcenter München, Referat für Arbeit und Wirtschaft der Landeshauptstadt München.
Purpose: analysis of trends in each district to plan resources and aid for unemployed citizens, and to design additional support such as seminars tailored to each district.
Presentation: a visual presentation of Munich’s districts and locations with concrete numbers and evidence.



The dataset contains the unemployment percentage for each district and for the city of Munich overall. It lets us examine each district and the trend of the unemployment rate over 24 years. The advantage over raw unemployment counts is that the percentage immediately shows the relation to population size. The analysis matters because it shows the history of Munich’s unemployment and how different years and policy choices coincide with different outcomes for the city.

title: Proposal
text:
Data quality issues

Dataset contains rows for both individual districts and the entire city total (Stadt München) in the same column → could filter out the total and create a separate subset.
Column Auspragung combines gender, nationality, and totals (insgesamt) within the same column (long format) → could split into separate subsets or reshape to wide format.

title: Proposal
text:
Plots


Show code
ggplot(arbeitslosen, aes(x = Indikatorwert)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Unemployment Rate",
    x = "Indikatorwert (Unemployment Rate in %)", y = "Count"
  )





Figure 1: Distribution of Unemployment Rate






Show code
selected_districts <- c(
  "03 Maxvorstadt",
  "19 Thalkirchen - Obersendling - Forstenried - Fürstenried - Solln",
  "16 Ramersdorf - Perlach",
  "04 Schwabing - West"
)

arbeitslosen %>%
  filter(Raumbezug %in% selected_districts) %>%
  filter(Auspragung == "insgesamt") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Jahr, y = Indikatorwert)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  facet_wrap(~Raumbezug, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Unemployment Trends over Time",
    subtitle = "Ramersdorf-Perlach consistently shows higher rates than Maxvorstadt",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Unemployment Rate (%)",
    caption = "Source: Statistisches Amt München"
  ) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = 7),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40")
  )





Figure 2: Ramersdorf-Perlach consistently shows the highest unemployment rates among the four districts, while Maxvorstadt remains the lowest across 2000–2024.

title: Proposal
section: RQ1: Unemployment trend by city area (2000–2024)
text:
RQ1: Unemployment trend by city area (2000–2024)
Specific claim: We estimate the trend of mean unemployment percentage over time (Indikatorwert ~ Jahr) grouped by city area and total, by calculating the annual average of the districts, and report line graphs showing the trend from 2000 to 2024.
Classify the analysis: Goal: Descriptive — we describe the state of Indikatorwert per city area from 2000–2024. Exploratory — after reviewing the available variables we fixed this question.
Model specification:

Response: Indikatorwert — unemployment percentage in % (<dbl>)
Predictor: Jahr — year (<dbl>), across six groups of Raumbezug (North, East, South, West, Center, Total)
Method: annual mean of the districts within each city area and city-wide total
Expected output: a summary dataset of annual means and line plots showing the trend from 2000 to 2024
Interpretation: this tells us how the unemployment rate has historically developed and shifted over a 24-year period across different regions of Munich

District → area mapping. Munich’s 25 districts don’t come pre-labeled with a compass region, so we assigned each district to North / East / South / West based on its approximate position relative to the city center. The two central districts (Altstadt-Lehel, Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt) are excluded from the area breakdown since they sit at the center rather than in any one direction; they remain included in the city-wide total.

Note: this mapping is a judgment call we made, not an official designation.



Show code
district_area <- tibble::tibble(
  district_code = c(
    1, 2, 3,                     # Center
    4, 11, 12, 24,                # North
    5, 13, 14, 15, 16,            # East
    6, 7, 17, 18, 19, 20,         # South
    8, 9, 10, 21, 22, 23, 25      # West
  ),
  area = c(
    rep("Center", 3),
    rep("North", 4),
    rep("East", 5),
    rep("South", 6),
    rep("West", 7)
  )
)

arbeitslosen_area <- arbeitslosen %>%
  mutate(district_code = as.numeric(str_extract(Raumbezug, "^[0-9]+"))) %>%
  left_join(district_area, by = "district_code") %>%
  filter(!is.na(area), Auspragung == "insgesamt")

annual_means_area <- arbeitslosen_area %>%
  group_by(area, Jahr) %>%
  summarise(mean_rate = mean(Indikatorwert, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

annual_means_total <- arbeitslosen %>%
  filter(Auspragung == "insgesamt", Raumbezug == "Stadt München") %>%
  transmute(area = "Total", Jahr, mean_rate = Indikatorwert)

annual_means_all <- bind_rows(annual_means_area, annual_means_total)




Show code
ggplot(annual_means_all, aes(x = Jahr, y = mean_rate)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.2) +
  facet_wrap(~area, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Unemployment Rate by City Area",
    x = "Year", y = "Mean Unemployment Rate (%)",
    caption = "Source: Statistisches Amt München"
  )





Figure 3: Mean unemployment rate by Munich city area, 2000-2024






Show code
library(plotly)

rq1_plot <- ggplot(annual_means_all, 
                   aes(x = Jahr, y = mean_rate, 
                       group = area, 
                       text = paste0("Area: ", area, "<br>Year: ", Jahr, "<br>Rate: ", round(mean_rate, 2), "%"))) +
  geom_line(color = "forestgreen", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.2) +
  facet_wrap(~area, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Unemployment Rate by City Area",
    x = "Year", y = "Mean Unemployment Rate (%)",
    caption = "Source: Statistisches Amt München"
  )

ggplotly(rq1_plot, tooltip = "text")




Figure 4: Interactive version of Figure 3 — hover over any point to see the exact year and mean unemployment rate for that area.


The interactive version lets readers hover over any point to read the exact year and mean unemployment rate for a given city area, rather than estimating values from the line alone.
Interpretation: (fill in once you’ve run this on the real data)

title: Proposal
section: RQ2: Gender differences in South Munich, 2024
text:
RQ2: Gender differences in South Munich, 2024
Specific claim: We compare mean unemployment percentage between männlich and weiblich groups (Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) across South Munich districts for the year 2024, using a difference-in-means test, and report the estimated difference, a 95% confidence interval, and a p-value.
Classify the analysis: Goal: Explanatory — we ask whether Auspragung (gender) is related to Indikatorwert. Exploratory — after seeing the data was already grouped by gender, we decided to investigate further.
Model specification:

Response: Indikatorwert — unemployment percentage in % (<dbl>)
Predictor: Auspragung — categorical grouping filtered to männlich & weiblich (<chr>)
Method: difference in means between the two groups, using a theory-based t null distribution
Expected output: the observed difference in means, a null-distribution plot with the observed statistic shaded, a p-value, and a 95% CI
Interpretation: this tells us whether male residents face systematically higher or lower unemployment percentages compared to female residents across South Munich districts in 2024



Show code
arbeitslosen |> count(Auspragung)


# A tibble: 5 × 2
  Auspragung       n
  <chr>        <int>
1 deutsch        650
2 insgesamt      650
3 männlich       650
4 nichtdeutsch   650
5 weiblich       650


Show code
arbeitslosen |> summarise(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) sum(is.na(x))))


# A tibble: 1 × 5
  `_id`  Jahr Indikatorwert `Basiswert 1` `Basiswert 2`
  <int> <int>         <int>         <int>         <int>
1     0     0             0             0             0



{infer} test


Show code
library(infer)

south_districts <- c(6, 7, 17, 18, 19, 20)

arbeitslosen_south <- arbeitslosen |>
  mutate(district_code = as.numeric(str_extract(Raumbezug, "^[0-9]+"))) |>
  filter(district_code %in% south_districts)

obs_stat <- arbeitslosen_south |>
  filter(Jahr == 2024 & Auspragung %in% c("männlich", "weiblich")) |>
  specify(Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) |>
  calculate(
    stat = "diff in means",
    order = c("männlich", "weiblich")
  )

null_dist <- arbeitslosen_south |>
  filter(Auspragung %in% c("männlich", "weiblich")) |>
  specify(Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) |>
  assume("t")

null_dist |>
  visualize() +
  shade_p_value(obs_stat = obs_stat, direction = "both")









Result interpretation: the difference in average unemployment rate between männlich and weiblich in 2024 falls within the high-probability region of the null distribution. The variation between groups can be explained by random sampling variation, and we cannot reject the null hypothesis that there is no systematic gender gap.


Show code
p_value <- null_dist |>
  get_p_value(obs_stat = obs_stat, direction = "both")

obs_stat


Response: Indikatorwert (numeric)
Explanatory: Auspragung (factor)
# A tibble: 1 × 1
   stat
  <dbl>
1   0.5


Show code
p_value


# A tibble: 1 × 1
  p_value
    <dbl>
1   0.617




Show code
boot_dist <- arbeitslosen_south |>
  filter(Jahr == 2024, Auspragung %in% c("männlich", "weiblich")) |>
  specify(Indikatorwert ~ Auspragung) |>
  generate(reps = 1000, type = "bootstrap") |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in means", order = c("männlich", "weiblich"))

ci <- boot_dist |>
  get_confidence_interval(level = 0.95, type = "percentile")

ci


# A tibble: 1 × 2
  lower_ci upper_ci
     <dbl>    <dbl>
1    0.210    0.809




Summary table


Show code
library(gt)

arbeitslosen_south |> count(Auspragung)


# A tibble: 5 × 2
  Auspragung       n
  <chr>        <int>
1 deutsch        150
2 insgesamt      150
3 männlich       150
4 nichtdeutsch   150
5 weiblich       150


Show code
arbeitslosen_south |> summarise(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) sum(is.na(x))))


# A tibble: 1 × 6
  `_id`  Jahr Indikatorwert `Basiswert 1` `Basiswert 2` district_code
  <int> <int>         <int>         <int>         <int>         <int>
1     0     0             0             0             0             0


Show code
arbeitslosen_south |>
  filter(
    Jahr == 2024,
    Auspragung %in% c("männlich", "weiblich")
  ) |>
  group_by(Auspragung) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_rate = mean(Indikatorwert, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_rate = min(Indikatorwert, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_rate = max(Indikatorwert, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean_rate, min_rate, max_rate), decimals = 1) |>
  fmt_integer(columns = n) |>
  cols_label(
    Auspragung = "Gruppe",
    n = "Bezirke (n)",
    mean_rate = "Mittelwert",
    min_rate = "Min",
    max_rate = "Max"
  ) |>
  tab_spanner(
    label = "Arbeitslosenquote (%)",
    columns = c(mean_rate, min_rate, max_rate)
  ) |>
  cols_align(align = "right", columns = where(is.numeric)) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Arbeitslosenquote nach Geschlecht",
    subtitle = "München, 2024"
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(md("Quelle: Statistisches Amt München"))
